lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators of staffed residential group homes that provide recurring supervision, personal care, habilitation, case support, and daily living assistance to children, disabled people without nursing care, or justice-involved residents under government, Medicaid, managed-care, or agency placement arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Donation-dependent orphanages, boot or disciplinary camps, private-pay atypical residences, temporary shelters, nursing facilities, assisted living, residential mental health or substance-abuse facilities, intellectual and developmental disability facilities classified elsewhere, correctional institutions, captive government operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring occupancy- or service-linked payments, commonly per-diem reimbursement, public-agency placement contracts, Medicaid or managed-care reimbursement, and block or capacity contracts, with residents receiving continuous supervision and personal care.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence to staffed group-home operators supported mainly by public or managed-care reimbursement. The full code also contains donor-funded orphanages, disciplinary camps, and atypical private-pay homes whose payer, ownership, transferability, and demand economics are too different for one screen; the narrowing is based on business-model comparability, not attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS measures the employment mix of Other Residential Care Facilities but not wage-weighted AI exposure, while O*NET describes occupation tasks across settings. I map high exposure to records, reports, case-plan drafting, scheduling, billing, compliance preparation, and routine management; moderate exposure to information synthesis in social-service roles; and low exposure to direct personal care, observation, crisis response, counseling, transport, and safety supervision. Employment shares are adjusted conceptually for the higher pay of managers and professional social-service staff. The all-industry task descriptions may overstate standardizable documentation in small group homes, while employment rather than payroll weighting biases the unadjusted mix downward where exposed professional work is better paid.
a.rationale: BLS shows that healthcare support, community and social service, and personal-care roles dominate employment. O*NET confirms that most aide and residential-advisor work requires in-person care, observation, relationship-building, safety rounds, first aid, and judgment, while meaningful documentation, case-plan, report, coordination, and administrative tasks remain exposed. The opportunity is therefore material but well below a majority of wage-weighted work.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-by-occupation extract plus time-and-motion studies separating direct presence, documentation, scheduling, case-plan preparation, billing, and compliance hours at LMM group-home operators would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The BLS matrix covers the full heterogeneous industry rather than the narrowed payer model.
O*NET tasks are occupation-wide and do not measure current AI substitution.
The wage adjustment is judgmental because the matrix reports employment rather than payroll by occupation.

rho.bridge: The Census survey measures any AI use across nonfarm businesses, not implementation of exposed labor opportunity in residential care. I start from recent economy-wide adoption near one-fifth, allow for five-year diffusion of documentation and back-office tools, and discount for fragmented provider systems, sensitive resident data, state-specific reporting, safeguarding, human review, and limited technical capacity. General business adoption likely overstates current readiness among small care providers but may understate future uptake through vertical care-management software.
rho.rationale: Operators can implement assisted case notes, shift summaries, incident-report drafting, schedule support, authorization checks, billing review, policy retrieval, recruiting administration, and management reporting. Implementation should remain partial because records affect vulnerable residents, benefits, courts, placements, and safety decisions; reliable human review and workflow integration are mandatory.
rho.change_evidence: A provider-level survey of deployed AI features, sustained user adoption, error and incident rates, saved labor hours, payer acceptance, and implementation cost across state-funded residential programs would materially change the value.
caveats: Economy-wide adoption does not establish care-provider implementation or labor avoidance.
State privacy, licensing, documentation, and incident-reporting requirements vary.
Savings from documentation assistance may be redeployed to resident care rather than reduce hiring.

e.rationale: A substantial part of the code consists of recurring residential supervision and personal-care services, but the eligible share is reduced by mission-driven or nonprofit organizations without conventional transferable equity, small single-site homes, government-controlled programs, donor-supported models, disciplinary camps, entities outside the target earnings band, and operators whose primary activity belongs in another residential-care code. The narrowed population retains multi-home or scaled group-home operators with external public or managed-care reimbursement and standalone control rights.
e.change_evidence: A stratified commercial-database review linking legal form, ownership, site count, payer mix, licenses, revenue recurrence, normalized EBITDA, and primary NAICS for sampled firms would materially change the eligible share.
caveats: No source measures eligibility under the narrowed ownership and earnings lens.
Legal entities may operate facilities across several neighboring residential-care codes.
The injected firm-count estimate uses a broad healthcare-facility margin bridge that may not represent publicly reimbursed group homes.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures intended sale or ownership transfer among employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers of licensed residential-care providers. I discount the broad intention rate for plans that do not close, nonprofit or mission-controlled ownership, licensure and payer approvals, and buyer sensitivity to compliance history. The broad employer survey likely overstates transferable equity availability in this field, though owner succession among qualifying for-profit operators may offset part of the discount.
s5.rationale: Qualifying transfers can occur when founder-led multi-home operators face succession, but conventional deal flow is constrained by nonprofit prevalence, state approvals, Medicaid enrollment, property and license structures, resident continuity, and serious diligence on incidents and compliance. Completed control deals should be materially below broad employer-owner intentions.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year database of control transactions involving NAICS 623990 group-home operators, matched to the eligible firm stock and supplemented by owner-age, legal-form, licensure-transfer, and failed-deal data, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions across industries rather than completed care-provider deals.
Nonprofit affiliations and asset transfers may not qualify as conventional control transfers.
Public transaction databases undercount small private and nonprofit combinations.

q.rationale: Per-diem and public-program reimbursement can let an operator retain administrative savings until the next rate review, but capture is limited by state rate setting, cost reporting, contract renewal, quality obligations, and chronic pressure to improve direct-care compensation and staffing. CMS is increasing transparency and requires a large minimum share of certain Medicaid personal-care payments to support direct-care compensation beginning within the horizon, reducing the scope to convert care-labor savings into durable profit. Back-office savings outside covered service payments may be retained more fully.
q.change_evidence: Contract and rate-file cohorts showing reimbursement methodology, cost-report treatment, direct-care spending tests, renewal resets, quality penalties, and realized margin after documented workflow savings would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The CMS direct-care spending rule applies to specified Medicaid services and has flexibilities, not every activity or payer in the narrowed lens.
State reimbursement and contract structures vary materially.
No source directly measures retention of AI-enabled benefits.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for the full Other Residential Care Facilities industry rather than constant-price demand for the narrowed group-home lens. I translate the modest industry employment increase into roughly flat five-year real service demand, then widen the range for state placement policy, Medicaid funding, disability and youth-service need, reimbursement adequacy, and movement toward less institutional settings. Employment may understate demand if labor shortages constrain staffed capacity and overstate it if staffing intensity rises because of regulation or acuity.
d5.rationale: BLS projects only modest growth for the industry, while Medicaid data show that policy increasingly favors home- and community-based services over isolated institutional settings. Small community group homes can qualify as community-based, but youth congregate-care reduction and individualized home supports can still reduce placements. Demand should be durable for high-acuity residents yet highly dependent on state policy and funded capacity.
d5.change_evidence: State-by-state five-year forecasts of licensed beds, funded placements, waitlists, occupancy, per-diem rates, closures, and transitions to family-based or self-directed care for the included populations would materially change this ratio.
caveats: Employment is a proxy for real service demand and can move with staffing rules or labor availability.
The BLS line covers excluded orphanage, camp, and atypical residential models.
National Medicaid HCBS trends do not identify this six-digit industry or distinguish qualifying group-home settings.

o.rationale: Residential supervision, personal care, safeguarding, crisis response, medication support, transport, and accountable case coordination require a licensed human service operator. Software can assist records, scheduling, communication, and monitoring but cannot independently house, supervise, or protect vulnerable residents. Operator displacement is mainly from family placements, self-directed supports, government insourcing, or migration to providers classified in neighboring care industries rather than from software-only supply.
o.change_evidence: Placement-flow data tracking transitions from licensed group homes to family care, self-directed services, independent housing with remote support, government provision, and technology-only alternatives would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required share of future service quantity.
Remote monitoring can change staffing models without eliminating accountable-provider demand.
Policy-driven reclassification or movement to home-based services can displace lens operators while resident need persists.

narrative.executive_view: Staffed group homes provide recurring, essential care to vulnerable residents, but the NAICS code is heterogeneous and much of the economics are shaped by public reimbursement, licensing, nonprofit ownership, and direct-care labor. AI can reduce documentation and coordination burden; it cannot replace continuous supervision, personal care, safeguarding, or relationship-based case work.
narrative.ai_impact: Useful applications include case-note and shift-summary drafting, incident-report support, schedule and authorization checks, billing review, policy retrieval, recruiting administration, care-plan synthesis, and management reporting. Human review remains necessary because outputs can affect resident safety, placements, legal proceedings, benefits, and regulatory compliance.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed or administered reimbursement can preserve some back-office savings between rate reviews. Capture is weaker where cost reports, contract renewals, direct-care spending requirements, understaffing, or payer pressure redirect savings toward wages and service quality rather than margin.
narrative.firm_availability: The code includes scaled group-home operators, but the transferable pool is diluted by nonprofits, government-controlled programs, single-site homes, mixed-code providers, and entities without conventional equity. Licensure, Medicaid enrollment, incident history, property structure, and resident continuity make diligence and closing more complex than ordinary service acquisitions.
narrative.demand_durability: Need for safe residential support remains durable for children and adults who cannot live independently, and direct human accountability is difficult to substitute. Growth is restrained by public budgets and deliberate movement toward family placements, self-directed supports, and less institutional settings; qualifying small community homes may benefit or lose share depending on state implementation.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the code's mixed populations and ownership forms, the absence of wage-weighted task data for the narrowed lens, and state-level variation in rates, licensing, placement policy, and transfer approval. Material operating risks include abuse or neglect allegations, critical incidents, workforce shortages, wage mandates, reimbursement delays, occupancy volatility, property separation, payer concentration, and compliance failures. The injected compensation ratio is directionally plausible for labor-intensive care but uses a vintage-mismatched denominator, while the injected firm count relies on a public healthcare-facility margin proxy that may misstate this provider model.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring publicly funded occupancy, persistent resident need, and heavy documentation and coordination burden create a focused opportunity for workflow assistance across multi-home operators.
narrative.principal_weakness: Direct human care and safeguarding dominate the service, while administered reimbursement, nonprofit ownership, and regulatory transfer constraints limit both benefit capture and the conventional acquisition pool.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 623990 Other Residential Care Facilities
sources.supports: Defines the industry as residential care with supervision and personal care, gives group-home and youth-care examples, and identifies neighboring excluded care industries.

sources.title: BLS National Employment Matrix: Other Residential Care Facilities
sources.supports: Provides the detailed industry occupation mix and projects total employment from 165.8 thousand to 170.0 thousand, including large healthcare-support, community-social-service, and personal-care groups.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Personal Care Aides
sources.supports: Describes direct personal care, health monitoring, companionship, household help, transport, case review, and client-record tasks.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Residential Advisors
sources.supports: Describes observation, safety rounds, first aid, mediation, rule enforcement, resident plans, counseling, reports, and administrative work.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Child, Family, and School Social Workers
sources.supports: Describes case records, reports, interviews, service plans, legal matters, counseling, referrals, coordination, and placement-related judgment.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports nationally representative recent business AI use near one-fifth overall, higher adoption among larger firms, and measurement across multiple administrative functions.

sources.title: Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services
sources.supports: Explains policy support for care in homes and communities and reports that 86.2 percent of Medicaid long-term-support users received HCBS in 2021.

sources.title: HCBS Payment Adequacy Provisions in the Ensuring Access to Medicaid Services Final Rule
sources.supports: Documents reporting, oversight, and the generally applicable 80 percent direct-care compensation minimum for specified Medicaid personal-care services beginning in 2030, with stated flexibilities and exceptions.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners intended to sell or transfer ownership within five years, providing a broad succession-intention proxy.
